userImpact: Decision branches become hard to scan when route ownership or branching direction drifts from Mermaid.
successCriteria: Decision node remains visually above both outcomes.
Yes and No branches stay clearly separated left-to-right.
Imported result stays in a renderer-backed Mermaid outcome when possible.

userImpact: Nested subgraphs look unreliable when parent boxes, child offsets, or containment structure drift.
successCriteria: Outer and inner containers keep stable nesting relationships.
Child nodes stay inside their intended subgraph.
Container geometry remains visibly intentional rather than auto-fit noise.

userImpact: Explicit Mermaid section ids must map cleanly into editable sections or users lose trust in imported structure.
successCriteria: Explicit subgraph id stays stable through import.
Section title and bounds remain readable.
Nodes remain attached to the correct imported section.

userImpact: Modern Mermaid ids with dots are common in infrastructure diagrams and break trust fast when labels or identity drift.
successCriteria: Dotted ids survive reconciliation intact.
Imported node labels remain readable without overlap.
Result does not silently degrade identity semantics.

userImpact: Even the simplest flowchart must remain visually obvious or all more complex imports feel risky.
successCriteria: Top-to-bottom order remains obvious at a glance.
Node spacing stays compact and readable.
Baseline case remains exact Mermaid layout.

userImpact: Cyclic flows like retry / verify loops are a flowchart staple; collapsing the back-edge breaks the user's intent.
successCriteria: All three forward edges and the back-edge survive import.
No node is silently merged or dropped because of the cycle.
Back-edge remains visually distinct from forward path.

userImpact: Self-loops are how users represent retry-until-success; if they disappear on import the flow loses its meaning.
successCriteria: Self-edge on the retry node survives import.
Downstream Done node remains connected.
No spurious extra nodes appear.

userImpact: Dispatcher / fan-out patterns are common in pipeline diagrams; collapsing six siblings into a stack destroys the visual.
successCriteria: All six children remain distinct nodes.
Each child stays directly connected to the dispatcher.
Children read as a row (or visually-grouped fan) under the dispatcher.

userImpact: Real-world labels carry commas, ampersands, and periods; mishandling them either silently drops the label or breaks parsing.
successCriteria: Quoted labels render with their punctuation intact.
Comma and period survive without HTML-entity escaping leaking through.
Node identity remains stable across import.
Ampersand survives inside quoted labels (e.g. 'User & Auth') without being misread as a fan-out separator.

userImpact: Distinct edge styles (thick / dotted / open) carry semantic meaning; collapsing them all to default removes the author's intent.
successCriteria: Thick (==>) edge stays visually heavier than default.
Dotted (-.->) edge keeps its dash pattern.
Open connector (---) remains unarrowed.

userImpact: Two HTTP methods between client and server should both render; merging them or losing the labels makes the diagram lie.
successCriteria: Both GET and POST edges remain present.
Edge labels stay attached to the correct edge.
Edges visually separate rather than overlapping.

userImpact: LR pipelines + classDef styling are how authors signal hot/cold paths; mishandling either breaks the visual story.
successCriteria: Layout flows left to right, not top to bottom.
Classed node retains its style hint (or a documented graceful fallback).
Three nodes import in the source order.

userImpact: Long linear chains are common in CI/CD and onboarding flows; any reshuffle breaks the obvious step-by-step read.
successCriteria: Six steps remain in original order.
Each step has a single inbound and outbound edge (except endpoints).
Total bounding box does not balloon disproportionately.
